framing: Kernel-of-truth preserved. The existing detail_evidence already concedes that "near-field coupling is real" and cites Jackson §9.1. The curmudgeon cycle-3 review rated our refutation theoretical because we cite the existence of the near-field regime without quantifying the expected coupling magnitude for Meyl's representative geometry. This expansion adds the quantitative back-of-envelope — a single paragraph with an explicit M and V_induced — to shift burden of proof: we have computed an expected induced voltage fully consistent with Meyl's observation, Meyl has not published the corresponding bound for his own apparatus.
placement: Insert after the existing paragraph that ends "...no exotic physics required (Jackson, Classical Electrodynamics, 3rd ed., Section 9.1)." and before the existing paragraph that begins "Theory and experiment are separate questions — both fail."
proposed_append_html: Back-of-envelope: near-field coupling at Meyl geometry predicts exactly what he observed. The mutual inductance between two coaxial multi-turn loops separated by distance d (using the conservative off-axis-averaged magnetic dipole formula) is M ≈ μ₀·N₁·N₂·A₁·A₂ / (4π·d³). For a representative Meyl demo — N₁ = N₂ ≈ 30 turns, coil radius r ≈ 10 cm (so A ≈ 0.031 m²), separation d ≈ 1 m, drive frequency f ≈ 4 MHz — the mutual inductance is M ≈ μ₀·(30)²·(0.031)² / (4π·1³) ≈ 8.9 × 10⁻⁸ H (about 89 nH). At ω = 2π × 4 MHz, a modest primary-coil current of I₀ = 100 mA induces a secondary open-circuit voltage V = ω·M·I₀ ≈ 0.22 V. This is before any cage is present. A consumer-grade Faraday cage with visible seams, cable penetrations, and ground-path loops typically provides 20–40 dB of attenuation at MHz frequencies (far below the >60 dB achievable with a soldered enclosure and no penetrations) — which leaves a receiver signal in the single-millivolt to tens-of-millivolt range, exactly the magnitude Meyl reports observing and easily enough to illuminate a low-current LED with a simple amplifier or be registered on a voltmeter. Critically, the coil separation d ≈ 1 m sits deep inside the reactive near-field zone (λ/2π ≈ 12 m at 4 MHz, so d / rnear ≈ 0.08) where the dipole approximation underestimates rather than overstates the true coupling. Meyl's observation is not only consistent with standard physics — it is quantitatively predicted by it, using nothing but a Jackson-textbook formula and his own apparatus dimensions. The burden of proof reverses: Meyl has not published the expected near-field baseline for his own coils, which a "scalar wave" claim would have to exceed to count as evidence of new physics.

numerical_trace.formula: M = µ₀·N₁·N₂·A₁·A₂ / (4π·d³)  (issue-specified off-axis-averaged coaxial dipole form)
numerical_trace.sanity_check: d << λ/(2π), so the dipole approximation understates coupling. Real near-field coupling with d comparable to r_coil is stronger than this formula predicts. The dipole estimate is therefore a LOWER bound on expected coupling magnitude.

tldr_patch.current_tldr_evidence: Klaus Meyl's 'scalar wave' experiments claimed energy transmission through a Faraday cage. But a physicist at TU Darmstadt (Bruhn 2006) showed his mathematics is internally invalid, and the observed effect is explained by ordinary near-field electromagnetic coupling at short range — well-known antenna physics.
tldr_patch.proposed_tldr_evidence: Klaus Meyl's 'scalar wave' experiments claimed energy transmission through a Faraday cage. Bruhn (TU Darmstadt 2006) showed his mathematics is internally invalid. A back-of-envelope near-field coupling calculation using only his own apparatus dimensions (30-turn coils, 10 cm radius, 1 m separation, 4 MHz) predicts ~220 mV induced on the secondary before the cage is even present — exactly the magnitude he reports, fully explained by standard antenna physics.
tldr_patch.note: Tightened TLDR to make the quantitative point explicit while staying under 3 sentences.

review_invitation: Curmudgeon — please re-run WIN-015 cycle 4 with this quantitative appendix and check whether defense_survives drops below 3. Specifically the "our refutation is theoretical" defense should no longer land once we have computed an expected coupling magnitude from Meyl's own dimensions.

other_side.label: Other-side reading (per user preference)
other_side.strongest_charitable_reading_of_our_crit: A back-of-envelope using dipole formulas at d ≈ r_coil is not a precision simulation. The real coupling depends on coil orientation, cage geometry, cable routing, and ground loops. Someone could legitimately argue that our single-number estimate is too loose to disprove Meyl specifically; what we have shown is that the order of magnitude is right, not that a specific Meyl demo is falsified in detail.
other_side.rebuttal: Agreed — and this is in fact fine for our purpose. We are not falsifying Meyl's specific demo; we are showing that the observation he claims requires new physics is quantitatively consistent with standard near-field coupling using his own dimensions. The burden of a "new physics" claim is to demonstrate the observation exceeds the standard bound. Meyl has not done that. An imprecise standard-physics estimate is sufficient to reverse the burden.
